reasoning: The Lean statement formalizes each component of the informal statement exactly: X is a MetricSpace, K and F are sets in X, IsCompact K captures "K is compact", IsClosed F captures "F is closed", and Disjoint K F captures "K and F are disjoint". The conclusion `∃ δ : ℝ, δ > 0 ∧ ∀ p q, p ∈ K → q ∈ F → dist p q > δ` is the direct literal encoding of "there exists δ > 0 such that d(p,q) > δ if p ∈ K, q ∈ F". The quantifier structure and strict inequality match, and no extraneous hypotheses (e.g., nonemptiness) are imposed. This is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
